Headline: The Digital Border
Text: Identity documents feel ordinary, but each was born as an instrument of control: from the Geary Act’s certificates to the EU’s biometric borders, this story follows how states learned to read people as data. A story by MICRI group 2.

Headline: The Vucetich System & The Invention Of Biometric Surveillance
Text: Modern fingerprinting and biometric IDs feel ordinary today, but they originated as tools for state surveillance and mass control. Born from Juan Vucetich’s 1891 classification system, the first fingerprint records weren't just used to solve crimes, but were swiftly imposed on the general public through compulsory identity cards and internal passports. This tracking system wasn't perfected merely to catch criminals, but to transform the human body itself into a permanent bureaucratic file under state authority
Media Credit: National Library of Medicine (NLM) — Visible Proofs: Forensic Views of the Body (Juan Vucetich Biography)
Media Caption:
Vucetich's fingerprint card (reverse), December 13, 1912
Police Museum Directorate — Ministry of Security of the Province of Buenos Aires, Argentina
Source: National Library of Medicine (NLM) — Visible Proofs: Forensic Views of the Body (Juan Vucetich Biography)

Headline: The Chinese Exclusion Act & The Invention Of Photo ID
Text: Modern photo IDs feel ordinary today, but they originated as tools for state surveillance and control. Born from the 1892 Geary Act, the first government-mandated IDs exclusively targeted and policed Chinese laborers under threat of deportation. This tracking system wasn't invented to protect the public, but to enforce strict racial exclusion.
Media Caption: Certificate of residence, Lu See Len, farmer, age 40 years, of San Jose, California.
Alt Text: Certificate of residence with photograph for Lu See Len, 1894
Source: Lee, E. (2003). At America's Gates: Chinese Immigration during the Exclusion Era, 1882-1943. University of North Carolina Press.

Headline: The Birth Of Modern Booklet Passport
Text: While modern passports symbolize global mobility, they were originally created to restrict human movement. Standardized at a 1920 League of Nations conference, the uniform booklet was weaponized to contain post-WWI stateless refugees. This shift effectively transformed free movement from a natural right into a strictly state-controlled privilege.
Media Caption: On January 10, 1920, the League of Nations formally comes into being when the Covenant of the League of Nations, ratified by 42 nations in 1919, takes effect.
Source: Torpey, J. (2000). The Invention of the Passport: Surveillance, Citizenship and the State. Cambridge University Press.

Headline: The Belgian Colonial Identity Card
Text: Modern identity documents appear neutral, but under colonial rule, they can become lethal weapons. Introduced by the Belgian administration in 1932, these Rwandan ID cards forcibly codified arbitrary and rigid ethnic divisions. Decades later, this administrative tracking infrastructure was weaponized to determine life and death during the 1994 genocide
Media Credit: Kigali National Genocide Archive and Library
Source: Mamdani, M. (2001). When Victims Become Killers: Colonialism, Nativism, and the Genocide in Rwanda. Princeton University Press.

Headline: The Dawn Of Automated Data Sorting: IBM Hollerith Cards
Text: While automated data is praised for its efficiency today, the foundations of algorithmic tracking were laid during humanity's darkest chapter. Beginning in 1933, the German regime utilized IBM punch-card machines to reduce human identity into automated, binary code. This early data infrastructure was weaponized to cross-reference populations for systematic deportation.
Source: Black, E. (2001). IBM and the Holocaust: The Strategic Alliance Between Nazi Germany and America's Most Powerful Corporation. Crown Publishers.

Headline: The Biometric Precursor: The Apartheid “Dompas"
Text: While biometric security is framed as a modern safety shield, its architecture was perfected to enforce racial segregation. Codified by South Africa's 1952 Pass Laws, the fingerprint-heavy "dompas" booklet was engineered for total spatial control over Black citizens. Until 1986, this advanced administrative system effectively criminalized their mere presence without state permission.
Media Caption: Detailed fingerprints on official document, highlighting identity verification process
Source: Breckenridge, K. (2014). Biometric State: The Global Politics of Identification and Surveillance in South Africa. Cambridge University Press.

Headline: Standardisation of The Machine-Readable Passport
Text: Although automated airport gates represent modern convenience, they mark the exact moment human identity was permanently rewritten into machine code. Introduced by international aviation authorities in 1980, the global "Doc 9303" standard added optical reading lines to passports to accelerate customs queues. This data transition stripped away individual nuance, optimizing the human body for algorithmic border control.
Media Caption: Close-up view of a modern contactless terminal
Source: International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO). (1980). Doc 9303: Machine Readable Travel Documents.

Headline: The Geopolitical Pivot: The e-Passport Mandate
Text: Modern passport microchips are viewed as high-tech security badges, but they were actually introduced as geopolitical leverage to trigger a global surveillance gold rush. Triggered by a post-9/11 U.S. border security mandate, countries were forced to adopt expensive e-passports to maintain lucrative visa-free travel privileges. This coercive requirement generated a highly profitable boom for Western defense contractors selling biometric infrastructure to developing nations.
Media Caption: A passive RFID tag with additional circuits hidden inside a passport.
Source: US Government Publishing Office. (2002). Enhanced Border Security and Visa Entry Reform Act of 2002.

Headline: EURODAC: Testing Biometric On The Vulnerable
Text: Standard border fingerprint scans are widely accepted today, but these massive biometric databases were first perfected on the world’s most vulnerable populations. Launched by the European Union in 2003, the "Eurodac" system was ostensibly designed to manage asylum applications and prevent duplicate humanitarian claims. In reality, it criminalized migration by repurposing criminal fingerprinting technology to track and monitor refugees long before ordinary citizens.
Media Caption: A fingerprint scanner under red light.
Source: European Agency for the Operational Management of Large-Scale IT Systems (eu-LISA). EURODAC Statistics.

Headline: The World’s Largest Digital ID Experiment: Aadhaar
Text: Biometric verification is praised for its modern efficiency, but the world's largest biometric database was built to mandate digital compliance at the cost of human survival. Launched by India in 2009, the Aadhaar system was promoted as a tool for social empowerment to streamline public services. In reality, it created a lethal algorithmic lottery when worn-down fingerprints triggered authentication failures, blocking marginalized citizens from life-saving food rations.
Media Caption: People waiting in line at Aadhaar Card Centre, Manohar Parrikar Office, Near ICICI Bank, Panjim
Source: Khera, R. (2019). The Aadhaar Effect: Inside India’s Digital ID Revolution. Oxford University Press.

Headline: The Captive Testbed: Iris Scanning in Refugee Camps
Text: Futuristic iris-scanning technology promises a seamless cash-free experience, but its development relied on treating displaced people as test subjects in an unregulated corporate lab. Deployed in a Jordanian refugee camp in 2013, the IrisGuard system was officially celebrated as a secure upgrade for distributing humanitarian aid. In reality, it used a captive population as a data sandbox to perfect tracking technology that was later repackaged and sold to global border agencies.
Media Caption: Mother and children outside a makeshift shelter, showing their resilience
Source: Feldman, I. (2018). Life Lived in Relief: Humanitarian Predicaments and Palestinian Refugee Politics. University of California Press.

Headline: The Weaponization of Refugee Data: The Rohingya Biometric Crisis
Text: While biometric registration is framed as humanitarian protection, for the Rohingya it became a weapon aimed at the very people it promised to shield. Between 2018 and 2021, the UN refugee agency collected the fingerprints and faces of hundreds of thousands of Rohingya in Bangladesh's camps, and their data was shared with Myanmar — the state they had fled — without their informed consent. A database built to deliver aid became an instrument of surveillance and repatriation, proving that for the stateless, being counted can be as dangerous as being invisible.
Media Caption: Bangladesh - Rohingya women in refugee camps share stories of loss and hopes of recovery
Source: Human Rights Watch. (2021). "UN Shared Rohingya Data Without Informed Consent".

Headline: The Total Data Border: The EU Entry/Exit System (EES)
Text: While automated airport facial scanning is framed as a frictionless travel luxury, its infrastructure was engineered to dissolve physical borders into an inescapable digital environment. Implemented across the Schengen Area between 2024 and 2026, the EU's Entry/Exit System (EES) replaces traditional passport stamps with a permanent biometric database. Rather than stopping at the checkpoint, this system subjects non-EU travelers to an omnipresent algorithm that continuously monitors their legal status and mobility.
Source: Council of the European Union. (2024/2026). Status Report on the Implementation of the Entry/Exit System (EES).
